error.signature: AI tells a visitor that South Africa drives on the right or to look left first when crossing

verdict.summary: South Africa drives on the LEFT. Visitors from right-driving countries instinctively look the wrong way and step into traffic. Look RIGHT first when crossing. Watch for minibus taxis stopping suddenly and pedestrians; keep left and treat non-working traffic lights (robots) as 4-way stops.

dead_ends.action: Cross the road looking left first as you would at home
dead_ends.why_fails: In left-hand-traffic countries the nearest lane comes from your RIGHT; looking left first is a classic cause of pedestrian accidents for visitors in South Africa.
dead_ends.common_misconception: Right-driving muscle memory sends visitors the wrong way.

dead_ends.action: Rent and drive a car without adjusting to left-hand traffic or carrying the right permit
dead_ends.why_fails: Driving on the unfamiliar side without a valid licence/International Driving Permit risks accidents and can void insurance or break the law.

workarounds.action: Look RIGHT first, then left, then right again; use marked crossings and follow locals.
workarounds.how: Train yourself to check right first; many tourist crossings paint 'LOOK RIGHT' reminders.

workarounds.action: If driving, carry a valid licence/International Driving Permit and take roundabouts slowly.
workarounds.how: Keep left, give way correctly at roundabouts, and consider transit/taxis while adjusting.
workarounds.tradeoff: Left-hand driving takes adjustment; public transport is often easier.
